title: Your Body
description: Your Body is a pulp fiction by Unknown author. Among the 300,000 American pulp magazines seized by Customs in 1938 for glorifying violence.
descriptionParagraphs: Your Body is a pulp fiction by Unknown author. Among the 300,000 American pulp magazines seized by Customs in 1938 for glorifying violence.

Its interest lies in how censors blur depiction, endorsement, and imitation, treating a book's violent material as if it were already an act. As a pulp fiction, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in New Zealand. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Among the 300,000 American pulp magazines seized by Customs in 1938 for glorifying violence. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Your Body is a pulp fiction by Unknown author. Among the 300,000 American pulp magazines seized by Customs in 1938 for glorifying violence.
banningParagraphs: Your Body entered censorship debates as a pulp fiction associated with risk, sensational culture, and violence. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around incitement to violence and violence.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1938 in New Zealand, where Customs Department classified, prohibited, or restricted. Among the 300,000 American pulp magazines seized by Customs in 1938 for glorifying violence. Among the 300,000 American pulp magazines seized by Customs in 1938 for glorifying violence.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
